doc.title: Deferrals — cash moved first, adjust later
doc.event: Two December 31 adjustments: the studio paid $1,200 on Oct 1 for 12 months of insurance (3 months now used), and it received $600 on Dec 1 for a 3-month project (1 month now earned). Prepare both adjusting entries.

variants.label: Asset drains into expense as time passes

lines.label: the used slice
lines.why: Oct–Dec's coverage is consumed — 3/12 of the 1,200 becomes expense.
lines.trap: Not the full 1,200 — nine months of future coverage is still an asset you own.

lines.label: asset drains
lines.why: The asset shrinks from 1,200 to 900 — exactly the unused months remaining.
lines.trap: No Cash line! Cash moved back in October. Adjusting entries NEVER touch cash — that's their defining feature.

variants.label: Liability converts to revenue as work is done

lines.label: owe less work
lines.why: One month delivered — the liability shrinks from 600 to 400.
lines.trap: Debiting a liability DOWN, not recording an expense — read the account family before the direction.

lines.label: the earned slice
lines.why: Revenue follows the work: 1/3 done, 1/3 earned.
lines.trap: The December cash receipt was NOT revenue then; this adjustment is where revenue actually happens.

memorize.body: Deferral = cash FIRST, income effect LATER. Prepaid: Dr Expense / Cr the prepaid asset (used slice). Unearned: Dr Unearned Revenue / Cr Revenue (earned slice). Slice = amount × time used ÷ total time.
Adjusting entries never include Cash. If you've written Cash in an adjustment, it isn't an adjustment.

questions.prompt: $1,200 paid Oct 1 for 12 months of insurance. The Dec 31 adjusting entry debits Insurance Expense for…

distractors.feedback: The full 1,200 expenses only if ALL 12 months were used — only 3 have passed. 1,200 × 3/12 = 300; the other 900 stays in Prepaid Insurance.
No-cash-this-month doesn't mean no expense — three months of coverage were consumed. That mismatch is exactly why adjusting entries exist.

build.scaffold: Compute the slice (time used ÷ total time), then move it: asset→expense, or liability→revenue. Cash is always the decoy in this chapter.
